title: Boulder Lectures on Thermal Dynamics and Hydrodynamic EFTs
abstract: Fluctuating hydrodynamics emerges in essentially any local many-body system at nonzero temperature. Effective field theory (EFT) approaches enable the quantitative study of this emergence, providing a controlled framework to capture late-time observables. These lectures introduce the organizing principles behind equilibrium and out-of-equilibrium dynamics in these thermalizing systems. A central focus is the modern construction of these EFTs, which frames fluctuating hydrodynamics through the lens of "strong-to-weak" spontaneous symmetry breaking. Drawing examples from both high-energy and condensed matter physics, we show how this paradigm adapts to systems ranging from spin chains to relativistic quantum field theories, including models with generalized symmetries or symmetries with 't Hooft anomalies. Finally, we discuss UV/IR constraints on transport parameters -- viewed as the Wilson coefficients of hydrodynamic EFTs -- both in continuum and on the lattice.
summary: This lecture series details the modern construction of Effective Field Theories (EFTs) to describe the thermal dynamics of quantum many-body systems. It shows that hydrodynamics governs late-time behavior and develops rigorous constraints on transport parameters by combining principles of causality, unitarity, and symmetry breaking. This work provides a systematic framework for analyzing thermalization across diverse physical models.
main_problem: The paper addresses the fundamental challenge of developing nonperturbative tools to study the out-of-equilibrium dynamics of generic quantum many-body systems at finite temperature.
main_result: It establishes that fluctuating hydrodynamics emerges universally in local many-body systems at $T>0$, and it derives universal constraints on transport parameters using causality and unitarity bounds.
method: The primary methods involve constructing and constraining the thermal effective action ($W$) via derivative expansions, utilizing the Schwinger-Keldysh formalism, and applying analytic continuation techniques to Green's functions.
model_or_system: The analysis applies to a wide range of local many-body systems, including spin chains, metals, insulators, and relativistic Quantum Field Theories (QFTs), covering both equilibrium and non-equilibrium regimes.
key_observables: Dynamical correlation functions, conductivity $\sigma_{ij}(\omega)$, the thermal effective action $W$, and the energy density $\epsilon$ and pressure $P$.
important_parameters: Finite thermal correlation length ($\xi < \infty$), temperature ($T$), chemical potential ($\mu$), and transport parameters like diffusivity ($D$) and sound speed ($c_s$).
main_assumptions: The EFT approach assumes a finite thermal correlation length ($\xi < \infty$), and derivations often rely on causality and unitarity constraints derived from analytic properties of correlation functions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the difference between global and local equilibrium time scales, the Schwinger-Keldysh contour with sources, and the conflict between diffusive spreading and subluminal propagation.
paper_structure: The structure moves from introducing the necessity of EFTs for out-of-equilibrium dynamics, to deriving the thermal effective action $W$ from equilibrium principles, and finally to constraining the parameters within $W$ using causality and unitarity bounds.
why_interesting: The rigorous derivation of hydrodynamic behavior and the establishment of universal bounds on transport coefficients (like the Planckian bound) provide powerful theoretical tools applicable to understanding thermalization in complex quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
